title: Wealth Doesn't Buy Health. Why not?
dek: Across 217 countries, income predicts a lot. The remaining gap is where institutions matter most.

beats.label: Beat 1
beats.headline: Income predicts a lot, not everything
beats.body: The line is the benchmark. The distance to the line is the story.
beats.aria_summary: Global scatter introduces expectation line and residual distance.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l01
cards.body: Before any country appears, start with the pattern.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l02
cards.body: Richer countries usually live longer. That is one of the most stable patterns in global development data.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l03
cards.body: Now hold that picture for a second.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l04
cards.body: If this were a perfect world, every dot would sit on a smooth curve: one level of income, one expected level of survival. But real countries do not line up that neatly.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l05
cards.body: In 2010, income explains about two-thirds of cross-country variation in life expectancy in this dataset. That is substantial. It is also incomplete.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l06
cards.body: That remaining third is where countries with similar income end up with very different life chances.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l07
cards.body: Treat the line as a benchmark, not a verdict. The key quantity is the vertical distance between a country and that line.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l08
cards.body: Above the line means people are living longer than income alone predicts.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l09
cards.body: Below the line means they are living shorter lives than income alone predicts.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l10
cards.body: This is where scale matters.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l11
cards.body: A typical country is not exactly on the line. In 2010, the median absolute life-expectancy gap is about 2.7 years. So modest divergence is normal.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l12
cards.body: But the tails are not modest. One in ten countries sits more than about 7.8 years away from expectation. At the extremes, the spread is wider still: around +11 years on the high side and about -18 years on the low side.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l13
cards.body: Here is the premise:

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l14
cards.body: income is powerful,

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l15
cards.body: income is not destiny,

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l16
cards.body: and the gap is big enough to matter in real lives.

cards.headline: b1_curve_mechanism_l17
cards.body: Once that frame is clear, we can track countries the way this chart tracks them: not by rank alone, but by distance from expectation and movement over time.

beats.label: Beat 2
beats.headline: Rwanda bends the trajectory
beats.body: The reveal is time-based first, model-based second.
beats.aria_summary: Rwanda under-5 reveal and trajectory crossing above expectation.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l03
cards.body: That drop is the first fact.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l04
cards.body: The second fact is where this period starts. Rwanda enters the 2006-2015 window after a decade of state and health-system reconstruction following the mid-1990s collapse. The baseline is not a mature, fully resourced system. It is a country rebuilding capacity while still carrying severe mortality burden.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l05
cards.body: So the movement in this decade is not just "improvement happened." It is improvement happening from a difficult starting point, and happening across multiple frontline outcomes at once.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l06
cards.body: In this dataset:

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l07
cards.body: maternal mortality drops from 534 to 290,

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l08
cards.body: infant mortality drops from 62.6 to 31.4,

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l09
cards.body: under-5 mortality drops from 98.3 to 42.0,

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l10
cards.body: and life expectancy rises from 57.2 to 66.7.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l11
cards.body: More important than one endpoint is the trajectory relative to expectation. Rwanda starts slightly below the income-based line and moves above it by 2010, then farther above by 2015.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l12
cards.body: That does not prove one policy caused one outcome.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l13
cards.body: It does show the gap can move in the favorable direction for a sustained stretch, not only in a one-year spike.

cards.headline: b2_rwanda_trajectory_l14
cards.body: And it shows what "institutions matter" looks like in practice: the same income level can deliver different survival odds depending on how care is financed, organized, staffed, and reached by households.

beats.label: Beat 3
beats.headline: A second country confirms the pattern
beats.body: Rwanda is not a one-case narrative.
beats.aria_summary: Vietnam and Rwanda highlighted above the same expectation line.

cards.headline: b3_vietnam_brief_l01
cards.body: Rwanda is not the only dot on that side.

cards.headline: b3_vietnam_brief_l02
cards.body: In 2010, Vietnam sits roughly 11 years above its income-based life-expectancy expectation.

cards.headline: b3_vietnam_brief_l03
cards.body: Vietnam comes from a different regional and institutional path, but the signal is the same: above the line.

cards.headline: b3_vietnam_brief_l04
cards.body: Vietnam is also not a one-year outlier in this series. It starts the decade far above the line and remains far above it by 2015.

cards.headline: b3_vietnam_brief_l05
cards.body: That brief confirmation matters because it lowers the chance that Rwanda is a one-case narrative. We are looking at a pattern that can appear in more than one place, through more than one pathway.

beats.label: Beat 4
beats.headline: The richest country still sits below the line
beats.body: Money and delivery are different things.
beats.aria_summary: U.S. below-line position, decade persistence, and peer comparison cues.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l01
cards.body: Now shift to a country that should, on wealth alone, be difficult to place below the line.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l02
cards.body: In 2010, the United States records life expectancy around 78.5 years, while the income-based expectation is about 81.1.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l03
cards.body: The point is not that the U.S. is low-income and constrained. It is one of the richest countries in the dataset period.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l04
cards.body: And within a tight band of similarly wealthy countries, the U.S. sits near the bottom on life expectancy.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l05
cards.body: If this were only a one-year anomaly, the gap would quickly close.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l06
cards.body: It does not.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l07
cards.body: The U.S. life-expectancy distance from expectation is below the line in 2006, still below in 2010, and farther below again by 2015.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l08
cards.body: That gives us a decade pattern, not a snapshot.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l09
cards.body: The same pattern appears in child and maternal signals.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l10
cards.body: U.S. maternal mortality is flat at 14.0 in 2006 and 14.0 in 2015, while high-income peers decline on average.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l11
cards.body: U.S. infant mortality improves over the decade, but by less than the average high-income peer reduction.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l12
cards.body: U.S. under-5 mortality also improves, but again more slowly than peer averages.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l13
cards.body: One concrete comparator: Canada moves maternal mortality from 9.0 to 7.0 over the same period.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l14
cards.body: None of this means income is irrelevant. The U.S. still has resources most countries do not.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l15
cards.body: It means money and delivery are different things.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l16
cards.body: A country can spend heavily while the care system remains fragmented.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l17
cards.body: Coverage can expand while access still varies by geography, cost, and continuity.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l18
cards.body: A wealthy country can spend heavily and still leave avoidable gaps in prevention and early intervention.

cards.headline: b4_us_comparison_arc_l19
cards.body: In this chart language, that entire institutional complexity shows up as one simple geometric fact: the dot stays below the line.

beats.label: Beat 5
beats.headline: The negative tail is structural
beats.aria_summary: Equatorial Guinea deep underperformance and Sierra Leone maternal floor marker.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l01
cards.body: At the opposite end of the distribution, Equatorial Guinea shows how large the negative distance can become.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l02
cards.body: In 2010, life expectancy is about 55.9 years against an income-based expectation near 73.7.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l03
cards.body: That is not a small miss. It is a structural miss.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l04
cards.body: It is the kind of distance no income ranking can justify on its own.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l05
cards.body: Supporting indicators move in the same direction: infant and under-5 mortality are far worse than income-based expectation in the same year.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l06
cards.body: Then the floor appears.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l07
cards.body: Sierra Leone records maternal mortality of 1,630 per 100,000 in 2010.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l08
cards.body: That marks the floor of this distribution in 2010.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l09
cards.body: Different country profile.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l10
cards.body: Different income path.

cards.headline: b5_mirror_case_l11
cards.body: Same failure.

beats.label: Beat 6
beats.headline: Compression happens, divergence persists
beats.body: The spread tightens, but does not collapse.
beats.aria_summary: Global year scrubber with distribution strip compression over 2006 to 2015.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l01
cards.body: So what moves these gaps?

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l02
cards.body: From 2006 to 2015, global distance from the line compresses somewhat:

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l03
cards.body: median absolute gap moves from about 3.1 years to about 2.7,

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l04
cards.body: 90th-percentile distance drops from about 9.3 to about 7.3,

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l05
cards.body: and mean absolute distance also declines.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l06
cards.body: So the world in this dataset gets somewhat less dispersed relative to income expectation.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l07
cards.body: But that compression is partial, not transformational.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l08
cards.body: Large positive and negative divergences remain present in every year. The distribution does not collapse around zero.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l09
cards.body: This is not an abstract prompt. In this data, upward movers usually do not improve in only one place. They improve across connected survival pathways: maternal care, infant outcomes, under-5 outcomes, and life expectancy moving together rather than in isolation.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l10
cards.body: That pattern suggests system-wide improvement, not a single isolated intervention.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l11
cards.body: A useful way to read the chart is to ask three operational questions.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l12
cards.body: First, can people enter care early and reliably through primary care rather than only at crisis point?

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l13
cards.body: Second, when higher-acuity care is needed, do referral chains actually connect local facilities to district and hospital capacity in time?

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l14
cards.body: Third, does financing protect continuity, or do cost shocks force households to delay care until conditions are harder and more expensive to treat?

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l15
cards.body: Those are not ideological questions. They are system design questions, and they are measurable in outcomes over time.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l16
cards.body: The contrast across these countries makes that concrete.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l17
cards.body: Rwanda's movement above expectation is accompanied by broad frontline improvement.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l18
cards.body: The U.S. remains below expectation over a decade despite high aggregate resources.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l19
cards.body: Equatorial Guinea sits far below expectation, and Sierra Leone shows what the floor can look like when care systems fail.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l20
cards.body: None of this yields a universal recipe that copies cleanly from one country to another.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l21
cards.body: But it does narrow where to look: conversion capacity, referral reliability, and continuity in maternal-child pathways are where gains are most likely to compound.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l22
cards.body: That persistence is the governance burden: which arrangements move dots upward, which arrangements stall despite resources, and which reforms reduce the deadliest outcomes at the bottom.

cards.headline: b6_what_changes_gap_l23
cards.body: You can watch that burden accumulate or ease, year by year, on the same line.

beats.label: Beat 7
beats.headline: Progress is governable
beats.body: What happens next is a governance question, not a math question.
beats.aria_summary: Final global settle with constrained-hope close.

cards.headline: b7_close_l01
cards.body: The hopeful reading is not that progress is automatic. It is that progress is governable.

cards.headline: b7_close_l02
cards.body: What happens next is a governance question, not a math question.

bullets: Scope: 217 countries, 2006-2015, using transformed MDG-aligned data in website/data/.
Frame: country-year outcomes are compared to income-based expectations using life expectancy as the persistent benchmark.
This is descriptive, not causal. Residual gaps identify over- and under-performance but do not isolate mechanisms.
Country averages hide within-country inequality and measurement limits.
External context citations are documented in docs/story_context_endnotes_v5.md.
